subject: ap-government
title: SCOTUS Comparison — Katzenbach v. McClung (1964)
stimulus:
Beginning in 1927, the McClung family owned a restaurant that served barbecue and other foods to patrons. Their restaurant was located about eleven blocks from an interstate highway and obtained about half of its supplies from out of state. Even though the restaurant had an indoor seating capacity of over 200, it refused to seat African Americans and restricted them to take-out orders.

Congress passed the Civil Rights Act of 1964, which prohibited discrimination in places of public accommodation. It was passed based on economic activity, given the travel of African Americans between states. However, the McClungs sued to prevent the enforcement of the Civil Rights Act. They argued that because the restaurant purchased half of its food from an in-state supplier, Congress could not regulate it because its activities were local in nature.

After a district court ruled in favor of the restaurant, the federal government appealed to the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court, in a unanimous decision, held that the Civil Rights Act of 1964 was constitutional, upholding Congress' power to prohibit discrimination when it poses a significant burden to interstate commerce. The Court reasoned that because the restaurant purchased about half of its food from an out-of-state supplier and because Congress sought to resolve the problem of discrimination that was a national issue, Congress had the power to enact the legislation that regulated the restaurant.

parts.prompt: Identify the constitutional clause that is common to both United States v. Lopez (1995) and Katzenbach v. McClung (1964).
parts.scoring_notes: Only the Commerce Clause earns the point.

scoring_points.description: Identify the constitutional clause that is common to both United States v. Lopez (1995) and Katzenbach v. McClung (1964).

parts.prompt: Explain how the facts in United States v. Lopez and Katzenbach v. McClung resulted in different holdings.
parts.scoring_notes: First point requires accurate information about Lopez. Second point requires explaining how the factual differences between the two cases produced different Commerce Clause outcomes.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in United States v. Lopez and Katzenbach v. McClung resulted in different holdings. (1 point — describe relevant fact/holding about the required Supreme Court case)

required_elements: Lopez carried a firearm in a school zone.
alternatives.correct_example: Lopez carried a firearm in a school zone.

required_elements: In the events leading to United States v. Lopez, a student was found guilty of violating the Gun-Free School Zones Act of 1990.
alternatives.correct_example: In the events leading to United States v. Lopez, a student was found guilty of violating the Gun-Free School Zones Act of 1990.

required_elements: The Supreme Court held that the national government could not regulate an activity that was not commercial and not related to interstate activity.
alternatives.correct_example: The Supreme Court held that the national government could not regulate an activity that was not commercial and not related to interstate activity.

common_traps: Describing only one case without explaining the comparison does not earn the 2-point tier

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in United States v. Lopez and Katzenbach v. McClung resulted in different holdings. (2 points — correctly explains how facts of BOTH cases led to different/similar holdings)

required_elements: The regulation in Lopez was not subject to interstate commerce, so the Court held that was unconstitutional, while the Court held that the regulation in Katzenbach was constitutional because it dealt with an interstate commerce issue.
alternatives.correct_example: The regulation in Lopez was not subject to interstate commerce, so the Court held that was unconstitutional, while the Court held that the regulation in Katzenbach was constitutional because it dealt with an interstate commerce issue.

parts.prompt: Explain how the decision in Katzenbach v. McClung reflects the democratic ideal of natural rights.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain the specific connection between the Katzenbach ruling and natural rights — the idea that individuals possess inherent rights not to be discriminated against. Accept any accurate explanation.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the decision in Katzenbach v. McClung reflects the democratic ideal of natural rights.

required_elements: Natural rights means that all people have certain rights that cannot be taken away, and Katzenbach supports this ideal by opposing the practice of discrimination.
alternatives.correct_example: Natural rights means that all people have certain rights that cannot be taken away, and Katzenbach supports this ideal by opposing the practice of discrimination.

required_elements: The decision in Katzenbach gives Congress the ability to pass legislation prohibiting discrimination, which is a threat to natural rights.
alternatives.correct_example: The decision in Katzenbach gives Congress the ability to pass legislation prohibiting discrimination, which is a threat to natural rights.
